raw_case:
Classic biphasic pulmonary blastoma with endobronchial invasion in a 16-year-old male: A rare case report
Pulmonary blastoma (PB) is a rare and aggressive lung neoplasm, representing 0.25 %–0.50 % of lung cancers and characterized by biphasic histology with both epithelial and mesenchymal components. We report a case of a 16-year-old male non-smoker presenting with hemoptysis and a 2-month history of dry cough and exertional dyspnea. Imaging revealed an ill-defined density in the left lung, and bronchoscopy identified a budding mass in the left bronchus. Biopsy results showed malignant spindle cells, with immunohistochemical staining positive for cytokeratin, vimentin, and TTF1, confirming classic biphasic pulmonary blastoma (CBPB). Further CT scans indicated multiple metastases in the right lung and bones. The patient was referred for palliative chemotherapy due to the tumor's aggressive nature. This case highlights the clinical presentation and diagnostic challenges of PB with endobronchial invasion.

Highlights

•CBPB is a rare lung tumor that exhibits aggressive behavior.•CBPB may invade the bronchial causing endobronchial lesions in a few cases.•More research is needed on CBPB treatment guidelines.1

Introduction

Pulmonary blastoma (PB) is classified within the category of carcinomas with pleomorphic, sarcomatoid, or sarcomatous elements in the World Health Organization (WHO) classification of lung tumors [1]. This rare and aggressive neoplasm accounts for only 0.25 %–0.50 % of all lung malignancies and is characterized by a biphasic histology that includes both epithelial and mesenchymal components [2,3]. PB is further divided into three subtypes: classic biphasic pulmonary blastoma (CBPB), well-differentiated fetal adenocarcinoma (WDFA), and pleuropulmonary blastoma (PPB) [4]. Due to its rarity, no standard treatment guidelines have been established [2]. To our knowledge, there are two cases describing PB exhibiting endobronchial invasion, complicating both diagnosis and treatment [2,4]. In this report, we present a case of a 16-year-old male diagnosed with CBPB exhibiting endobronchial invasion.2

Case presentation

A 16-years-old non-smoker male presented to the pulmonary department with hemoptysis. He had a 2 months history of dry coughing and exertional dyspnea without any other medical condition. Physical examination of the chest did not reveal any abnormality. His routine blood tests were within normal range except for an elevated CRP level at 40mg/L. Chest X-ray showed density with ill-defined margins in the left lung extending from the left bronchus to the pleura. The tuberculosis skin test was negative and no response was noticed after getting clarithromycin 250mg OD twice a day and cefixime 200mg OD twice a day, both for 7 days as an empirical treatment for suspected pneumonia.CT scan revealed a circular density in the upper lobe of the left lung with calcification within it (Fig. 1). Afterward, a bronchoscopy showed a polypoid intraluminal mass in the left main bronchus (Fig. 2). A biopsy taken from the budding showed malignant spindle cells (Fig. 3). Immunohistochemical staining findings of the lung biopsy were positive (CK, Vimentin, TTF1), which is highly suggestive of CBPB (Fig. 4). Another contrast-enhanced CT scan revealed multiple metastases in the lower lobe of the right lung, as well as in the vertebrae of T6, T12, and S1, and in the pelvic bone (Fig. 5).The patient was referred to the oncology department to get palliative chemotherapy due to the highly aggressive tumor he had.3

Discussion

PB is a rare primary lung malignancy that occurs in only 0.25–0.5 % of all lung malignancies [2,5,6]. In 1945, Barnett and Barnard described the first case of PB [7]. Koss et al., have suggested the higher prevalence of CBPB in the 4th to 5th decades of life without any gender dominance [8]. In our case, the patient is 16 years old, which is uncommon for this type of tumor at this age.The pathogenesis of PB remains uncertain, one of the hypotheses is that PB originates from pluripotent pulmonary blastemas due to their resemblance to fetal lung tissue [9]. Another hypothesis is the mutations in b-catenin, which indicate a possible implication of the Wnt signaling pathway [5].CBPB is associated strongly with smoking, which is proposed with some cases demonstrating p53 mutation and the presence of products of tobacco smoke, adducts of benzpyrene in the tumor tissue [[4], [5], [6],8,9]. Our patient is a non-smoker, so other factors may have contributed to this tumor.PB is composed of an epithelial and a mesenchymal component, the appearance is similar to the gestational lung in the pseudo glandular phase less than 16 weeks [4,5]. Some cases of PB contain foci of osteosarcoma, chondrosarcoma, and rhabdomyosarcoma [5].Clinical presentation of CBPB, which is similar to symptoms of other non‐small cell lung carcinomas (NSCLC), is non-specific chest symptoms like cough (seen in 33 % of cases), dyspnea, hemoptysis (seen in 29 % of cases) or chest pain (seen in 33 % of cases) due to tumor compressing the bronchial and/or pleura [2,4,6,9,10]. Our patient presented with only a cough, hemoptysis and exertional dyspnea.Chest radiograph of PB may usually reveal a large, well border, homogenous, solitary lesion with smooth margins, in most cases is unilateral, and may completely opacify the hemithorax and cause mediastinal shift [4,6]. Chest CT scans may typically show a heterogeneous enhancing soft tissue mass with smooth margins and the presence of probable central areas necrosis of tumor [5,9]. MRI may reveal a heterogeneous mass, typically hyperintense on both T1 and T2 weighted sequences [9]. On imaging, it is very important to evaluate regional lymph nodes, metastases, and local invasion of the esophagus, bronchial tree, and heart [9].In CBPB, there is a predominance of involvement in the upper lobe [6,9]. Bronchoscopy and biopsy are useful diagnostic because endobronchial growth is present in 25 % of cases, and they are difficult diagnostic tools when a tumor invades the pleura [4,5]. However, ultrasound/CT-guided transthoracic biopsy can confirm the diagnosis [4,5]. The patient in our case, had endobronchial lesions invaded from a lung tumor, which requires further investigation to assess for CBPB diagnosis.To confirm the diagnosis, biopsies should be taken from multiple areas to confirm the presence of both epithelial and mesenchymal malignant components [9]. Immunohistochemistry is commonly used to diagnose PB because of the biphasic structure of this tumor [5,9]. The epithelial components stain positive for cytokeratin (CK), carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA), epithelial membrane antigen (EMA), thyroid transcription factor-1 (TTF-1), and surface protein alpha. However, the stromal components stain positive for vimentin, desmin, muscle-specific actin, myoglobin, and S-100 [5,9]. CBPB has a glandular component that is positive for keratin, CEA, globulin, and chromogranin, while the stromal component is positive for vimentin, actin, desmin, and myoglobin [6]. In our case, the biopsy taken by tru-cut from the left pulmonary mass, which is positive for CK, Vimentin, and TTF1, confirms the diagnosis. Therefore, histology combined with additional immunohistochemical tests can differentiate CBPB from other primary lung malignancies [6]. The differential diagnosis includes fetal adenocarcinoma, which is a monophasic tumor without primitive mesenchymal components [10]. Immunohistochemistry findings can differentiate between fetal adenocarcinoma and CBPB by predominantly membranous expression of β‐catenin and the absence of TTF1 expression in fetal adenocarcinoma [10]. PPB, as another potential differential diagnosis, may be associated with DICER1 gene mutations in approximately 40 % of cases [11]. Immunohistochemical of PPB showed positive staining of CK, CEA, and EMA in the epithelium. However, the mesenchyme showed positive staining for vimentin, desmin, and smooth muscle actin (SMA) [12]. Our patient did not undergo analysis for DICER1 gene mutations because it was not available in our country. The diagnosis was based on histopathological examination and immunohistochemical analysis, confirming the diagnosis of CBPB.There is no staging system for PB, and it may be reasonable to stage them like NSCLC [4]. Distant metastasis by hematogenous spread has been described to the brain, bone, liver, kidney, pancreas, and adrenal [4]. There are no serum tumor markers that specifically indicate the diagnosis of PB [2,5]. However, alpha-fetoprotein (AFP), β‐human chorionic gonadotropin (β‐HCG), and lactate dehydrogenase (LDH) levels can assist in identifying improvement or recurrence of the disease [10].Surgical resection is a common treatment in the early stages of PB diagnosis [5]. Lobectomy or pneumonectomy may be performed in cases of CBPB, along with the resection of any infiltrated organs, particularly the pleura and pericardium [9]. The role of adjuvant chemotherapy and/or radiotherapy is still unclear; however, it is often administered given the possibility of relapsing and can be used in cases of unresectable tumors [2,9,10]. Before surgical resection, neoadjuvant therapy may be administered for downstaging the tumor [5]. Cisplatin with etoposide has been proposed as an adjuvant chemotherapy for PB [4,5]. Treatment mainly includes chemotherapy when there are metastases [5]. In some cases of PB, high expression of PD-L1 has been reported, but no study of immunotherapeutic agent used has been published yet [5]. PB also has rich vasculature in the periphery of the tumor; thus, antiangiogenic agents, such as Anlotinib, may play an important role in inhibiting tumor growth [2,10].The prognosis of pulmonary blastoma is poor and CBPB is the poorest [5,9,10]. CBPB is typically rapidly progressed, with many patients presenting at an advanced stage [10]. CBPB has a 5-year survival rate of 16%–25 % and a 10-year survival rate of 8 % [9]. The patient was put on chemotherapy due to the aggressive nature of the tumor and its advanced stage.4

Conclusion

CBPB is a very rare lung tumor with aggressive behavior that may invade the bronchial in a few cases. Diagnosing this tumor in young patients is challenging, as it often presents at an advanced stage due to nonspecific symptoms such as cough. Despite many research papers on PB and its subtypes, there is currently no established standard treatment or TNM staging. Further research efforts are needed to provide treatment guidelines to improve patient outcomes.

CRediT authorship contribution statement

Yousef Alsaffaf: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Data curation, Conceptualization. Ahmed Aldolly: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Data curation. Hazem Arab: Writing – original draft. Nour Alsolaiman: Writing – review & editing. Asaad Jalil: Supervision, Investigation. Simon Youssef: Supervision, Investigation. Fariz Ahmad: Supervision, Investigation.

Declaration of competing interest

The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

generate_case.case_summary:
- Patient Information: 16-year-old male, non-smoker.
- Chief Complaint: Hemoptysis.
- History of Present Illness: Two months of dry coughing and exertional dyspnea.
- Past Medical History: None.
- Personal History: Non-smoker; no significant personal history.
- Family History: Not specified.
- Physical Examination: Unremarkable chest examination.
- Ancillary Tests:
  - Bloodwork: Normal except for elevated CRP (40 mg/L).
  - Chest X-ray: Left lung density with ill-defined margins extending to the pleura.
  - CT scan: Circular density in the left upper lung with calcification; metastases in the right lung, vertebrae (T6, T12, S1), and pelvic bone.
  - Bronchoscopy: Polypoid intraluminal mass in the left main bronchus.
  - Biopsy: Malignant spindle cells.
  - Immunohistochemical staining: Positive for cytokeratin (CK), vimentin, and TTF-1, confirming classic biphasic pulmonary blastoma (CBPB).
generate_case.differential_diagnosis:
1. **Initial Considerations**:
   - Based on the presentation of hemoptysis, cough, and exertional dyspnea, primary pulmonary malignancies including classic biphasic pulmonary blastoma (CBPB), well-differentiated fetal adenocarcinoma, and pleuropulmonary blastoma were considered.
   - Infectious causes like tuberculosis or pneumonia were also initially suspected but were ruled out due to a negative tuberculosis skin test, lack of response to antibiotics, and imaging features inconsistent with infection.

2. **Diagnostic Testing**:
   - The biopsy and immunohistochemical testing confirmed biphasic histology (epithelial and mesenchymal components), distinguishing CBPB from other conditions.
   - CBPB was favored due to spindle cell morphology and immunohistochemical positivity for CK, vimentin, and TTF-1.
   - Other differential diagnoses, like fetal adenocarcinoma, were excluded because of the lack of TTF-1 expression in fetal adenocarcinoma and the absence of primitive mesenchymal components.
   - Pleuropulmonary blastoma (PPB) was excluded due to histological findings not suggesting DICER1 gene-related mutations, though genetic testing was unavailable.

3. **Consideration of Metastatic Spread**:
   - The presence of metastases in the right lung and bones confirmed the tumor’s aggressive behavior.
generate_case.final_diagnosis: The final diagnosis is **classic biphasic pulmonary blastoma (CBPB)** with endobronchial invasion and metastatic spread. This is supported by biopsy findings, immunohistochemical staining, and clinical presentation consistent with an aggressive biphasic lung neoplasm.

results.content:
### Resoning:
<step 1> Analyze the Chief Complaint: Hemoptysis is the primary symptom. This indicates bleeding from the respiratory tract, which is a serious symptom requiring investigation, especially in a young individual.

<step 2> Consider the History of Present Illness: Two months of dry cough and exertional dyspnea suggest a subacute or chronic respiratory issue. Dry cough points towards irritation or inflammation without significant mucus production. Exertional dyspnea indicates impaired respiratory function, possibly due to airway obstruction or lung tissue involvement.

<step 3> Review Past Medical and Personal History: The patient has no past medical history and is a non-smoker. This reduces the likelihood of common smoking-related lung diseases and other pre-existing conditions as primary causes.

<step 4> Evaluate Physical Examination Findings: An unremarkable chest examination is somewhat non-specific but doesn't rule out underlying pathology, especially if the lesions are not causing significant changes detectable by auscultation and percussion at the time of examination.

<step 5> Analyze Bloodwork Results: Elevated CRP (40 mg/L) indicates systemic inflammation. This is a non-specific marker but supports the presence of an underlying pathological process.

<step 6> Interpret Chest X-ray Findings: Left lung density with ill-defined margins is suggestive of a lung lesion. "Density" indicates an area of increased opacity compared to normal lung tissue, and "ill-defined margins" raise suspicion for malignancy or inflammatory conditions. Extension to the pleura suggests pleural involvement.

<step 7> Interpret CT Scan Findings: The CT scan provides crucial details. A "circular density in the left upper lung with calcification" is highly suggestive of a mass, and calcification can be seen in both benign and malignant lesions, but in the context of other findings, malignancy becomes more concerning. The presence of metastases in the right lung, vertebrae, and pelvic bone signifies advanced, likely malignant, disease.

<step 8> Analyze Bronchoscopy Findings: A polypoid intraluminal mass in the left main bronchus directly visualizes a tumor in the airway. This explains the hemoptysis as the mass can be friable and bleed, especially with coughing.

<step 9> Interpret Biopsy and Immunohistochemistry Results: The biopsy revealing "malignant spindle cells" confirms malignancy. The immunohistochemistry showing positivity for cytokeratin (CK), vimentin, and TTF-1 is key for specific tumor typing.
    - Cytokeratin (CK) positivity indicates epithelial origin.
    - Vimentin positivity is seen in mesenchymal tumors and some epithelial tumors, especially spindle cell types or those with sarcomatoid features.
    - TTF-1 positivity is strongly associated with lung adenocarcinoma and, importantly, Pulmonary Blastoma.

<step 10> Synthesize all findings to reach a Final Diagnosis: Considering the patient's age (16 years), the location of the primary tumor in the lung, the presence of metastases, the spindle cell morphology, and the specific immunohistochemical profile (CK+, Vimentin+, TTF-1+), the diagnosis of **Classic Biphasic Pulmonary Blastoma (CBPB)** is definitively confirmed, as stated by the immunohistochemistry results. This rare lung tumor is known to occur in children and young adults and can present with metastases at diagnosis.

### Answer:
Classic biphasic pulmonary blastoma (CBPB)

reasoning_eval.step: Analyze the Chief Complaint: Hemoptysis is the primary symptom. This indicates bleeding from the respiratory tract, which is a serious symptom requiring investigation, especially in a young individual.

Consider the History of Present Illness: Two months of dry cough and exertional dyspnea suggest a subacute or chronic respiratory issue. Dry cough points towards irritation or inflammation without significant mucus production. Exertional dyspnea indicates impaired respiratory function, possibly due to airway obstruction or lung tissue involvement.

Review Past Medical and Personal History: The patient has no past medical history and is a non-smoker. This reduces the likelihood of common smoking-related lung diseases and other pre-existing conditions as primary causes.

Evaluate Physical Examination Findings: An unremarkable chest examination is somewhat non-specific but doesn't rule out underlying pathology, especially if the lesions are not causing significant changes detectable by auscultation and percussion at the time of examination.
Analyze Bloodwork Results: Elevated CRP (40 mg/L) indicates systemic inflammation. This is a non-specific marker but supports the presence of an underlying pathological process.

Interpret Chest X-ray Findings: Left lung density with ill-defined margins is suggestive of a lung lesion. "Density" indicates an area of increased opacity compared to normal lung tissue, and "ill-defined margins" raise suspicion for malignancy or inflammatory conditions. Extension to the pleura suggests pleural involvement.

Interpret CT Scan Findings: The CT scan provides crucial details. A "circular density in the left upper lung with calcification" is highly suggestive of a mass, and calcification can be seen in both benign and malignant lesions, but in the context of other findings, malignancy becomes more concerning. The presence of metastases in the right lung, vertebrae, and pelvic bone signifies advanced, likely malignant, disease.

Analyze Bronchoscopy Findings: A polypoid intraluminal mass in the left main bronchus directly visualizes a tumor in the airway. This explains the hemoptysis as the mass can be friable and bleed, especially with coughing.

Interpret Biopsy and Immunohistochemistry Results: The biopsy revealing "malignant spindle cells" confirms malignancy. The immunohistochemistry showing positivity for cytokeratin (CK), vimentin, and TTF-1 is key for specific tumor typing.
    - Cytokeratin (CK) positivity indicates epithelial origin.
    - Vimentin positivity is seen in mesenchymal tumors and some epithelial tumors, especially spindle cell types or those with sarcomatoid features.
    - TTF-1 positivity is strongly associated with lung adenocarcinoma and, importantly, Pulmonary Blastoma.

Synthesize all findings to reach a Final Diagnosis: Considering the patient's age (16 years), the location of the primary tumor in the lung, the presence of metastases, the spindle cell morphology, and the specific immunohistochemical profile (CK+, Vimentin+, TTF-1+), the diagnosis of **Classic Biphasic Pulmonary Blastoma (CBPB)** is definitively confirmed, as stated by the immunohistochemistry results. This rare lung tumor is known to occur in children and young adults and can present with metastases at diagnosis.

<Step 2> Infectious causes like tuberculosis or pneumonia were initially suspected but were ruled out due to a negative tuberculosis skin test, lack of response to antibiotics, and imaging features inconsistent with infection.
<Step 3> Biopsy and immunohistochemical testing confirmed biphasic histology (epithelial and mesenchymal components), distinguishing CBPB from other conditions.
<Step 4> CBPB was favored due to spindle cell morphology and immunohistochemical positivity for CK, vimentin, and TTF-1.
<Step 5> Other differential diagnoses, like fetal adenocarcinoma, were excluded because of the lack of TTF-1 expression in fetal adenocarcinoma and the absence of primitive mesenchymal components.
<Step 6> Pleuropulmonary blastoma (PPB) was excluded due to histological findings not suggesting DICER1 gene-related mutations, though genetic testing was unavailable.
<Step 7> The presence of metastases in the right lung and bones confirmed the tumor’s aggressive behavior.

<Step 8> The final diagnosis is classic biphasic pulmonary blastoma (CBPB) with endobronchial invasion and metastatic spread, supported by biopsy findings, immunohistochemical staining, and clinical presentation consistent with an aggressive biphasic lung neoplasm.
